event: Hunter-gatherers reach Cyprus, leaving traces at sites such as Aetokremnos on the Akrotiri peninsula.
Neolithic farming communities establish permanent villages, notably at Khirokitia with its distinctive round stone dwellings.
The Chalcolithic period begins as copper working emerges, foreshadowing the metal that would give the island its name.
The Bronze Age opens and Cypriot copper becomes a prized commodity traded across the eastern Mediterranean.
City-kingdoms flourish and Cyprus, identified with 'Alashiya' in ancient texts, exports copper to Egypt and the Near East.
Mycenaean Greek settlers arrive after the Bronze Age collapse, beginning the island's lasting Hellenization.
Phoenicians establish a colony at Kition (modern Larnaca), adding to the island's mix of cultures.
Cypriot city-kingdoms submit to the Assyrian king Sargon II, commemorated on a stele set up at Kition.
Egypt under Pharaoh Amasis II briefly brings Cyprus under its control.
Cyprus comes under the Persian Achaemenid Empire as part of its fifth satrapy.
Cypriot cities join the Ionian Revolt against Persia but are subdued the following year.
Evagoras I becomes king of Salamis and works to unify the island while promoting Greek culture.
Cypriot kings ally with Alexander the Great, ending Persian rule over the island.
Cyprus is absorbed into the Ptolemaic Kingdom of Egypt, abolishing the old city-kingdoms.
Rome annexes Cyprus, attaching it administratively to the province of Cilicia.
The apostles Paul and Barnabas evangelize Cyprus, converting the Roman proconsul Sergius Paulus.
A devastating Jewish revolt under Trajan ravages the island, after which Jews are barred from Cyprus.
Gothic raiders attack Cyprus during the wider crisis of the third-century Roman Empire.
Cyprus passes to the Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire as Constantinople becomes the imperial capital.
The Church of Cyprus is granted autocephaly (self-governance) by the Council of Ephesus.
Arab fleets under Muawiyah raid Cyprus, beginning centuries of contested Arab-Byzantine condominium.
Emperor Nikephoros II Phokas fully restores Byzantine control over Cyprus.
King Richard the Lionheart conquers Cyprus during the Third Crusade and later sells it on.
Guy of Lusignan acquires Cyprus, founding the French Crusader Lusignan Kingdom that ruled for nearly three centuries.
After the fall of Acre, Cyprus becomes the principal refuge and base for Latin Christendom in the East.
Queen Caterina Cornaro cedes Cyprus to the Republic of Venice, which fortifies the island against the Ottomans.
Ottoman forces invade Cyprus and capture Nicosia after a brutal siege.
The fall of Famagusta completes the Ottoman conquest, beginning over three centuries of Turkish rule.
The Orthodox Archbishop of Cyprus is recognized by the Ottomans as ethnarch, leader of the Greek Cypriot community.
Ottoman authorities execute Archbishop Kyprianos and other Greek Cypriot leaders amid the Greek War of Independence.
Britain takes administrative control of Cyprus under the Cyprus Convention while it remains nominally Ottoman.
Britain annexes Cyprus outright after the Ottoman Empire enters World War I on the opposing side.
Cyprus is formally declared a British Crown Colony.
Greek Cypriot demands for enosis (union with Greece) erupt in the Oktovriana uprising against British rule.
The EOKA guerrilla campaign launches an armed struggle against British rule for union with Greece.
Cyprus gains independence from Britain, with Archbishop Makarios III as its first president.
Intercommunal violence erupts after Makarios proposes constitutional changes, displacing many Turkish Cypriots.
The United Nations deploys the UNFICYP peacekeeping force, which remains on the island for decades.
A Greek junta-backed coup is followed by a Turkish military invasion that partitions the island.
Turkish Cypriots unilaterally declare the Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus, recognized only by Turkey.
Crossings along the Green Line dividing the island are partially opened for the first time since 1974.
The UN's Annan reunification plan is rejected by Greek Cypriots, and Cyprus joins the European Union still divided.
Cyprus adopts the euro as its currency, replacing the Cypriot pound.
A severe banking crisis forces a controversial EU bailout that imposes losses on large depositors.
UN-led reunification talks at Crans-Montana collapse without a settlement, leaving the island divided.
